lens.included_activities: Government establishments planning, administering, and coordinating public health programs and services, including environmental and mental health programs, health statistics, immunization programs, and public-health inspections.
lens.excluded_activities: Government hospitals and clinics, agricultural-product inspection, private consultants, software vendors, laboratories, and nonprofit technical-assistance organizations are excluded because they are classified outside 923120; captive units and non-control financing vehicles are also excluded.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The establishments are government agencies funded through appropriations, taxes, intergovernmental grants, and program-specific public funding rather than recurring commercial fees paid by external customers.

a.bridge: CDC reports that adopting state, tribal, local, and territorial public-health agencies use generative AI for document drafting, meeting summaries, coding, communications, and chatbots, while GAO describes a workforce spanning service delivery, emergency response, administration, and other functions. I map the documented text, data, and operational-support use cases to the wage-weighted share of current tasks in 923120, then discount for already-adopted tools and for inspections, emergency response, scientific judgment, stakeholder work, and accountable decisions. The agency examples likely overstate exposure for less digitally mature jurisdictions, while omission of non-generative analytics may understate it.
a.rationale: Public-health administration contains meaningful report drafting, communication, grant administration, meeting synthesis, data preparation, coding, and routine inquiry work that AI can substitute or use to avoid hiring. The larger share of scientific interpretation, field inspection, emergency coordination, legal process, and public accountability remains human-intensive.
a.change_evidence: A representative time-use study linked to payroll by occupation across state and local health departments, with task-level measurement of existing automation, would materially change this range.
caveats: CDC describes use cases among agencies that have adopted AI, not a wage-weighted task census of all 923120 establishments.
Government job classifications and digital maturity vary substantially by jurisdiction.
The frozen labor-share input is missing, so this task-share estimate cannot establish an industry-level labor-benefit magnitude by itself.

rho.rationale: Document, communication, meeting, coding, and intake aids can be deployed within five years, and CDC reports that some public-health agencies already use them. Implementation is capped by procurement cycles, fragmented legacy systems, privacy and security rules, scientific validation, governance, workforce training, and mandatory human review.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of state and local health departments reporting production deployment, user adoption, hours saved, validation failure rates, and security approvals by workflow would materially change this estimate.
caveats: Reported adopters are likely more capable than the median jurisdiction.
Time savings reported by agencies are not a measured conversion rate from exposed work to sustained operational implementation.
The estimate excludes pricing, public funding, demand, and valuation effects.

e.rationale: The Census definition limits 923120 to government establishments, while the screen requires LMM firms supplying outsourced services to external customers. With the frozen LMM-firm count also unavailable, the denominator for an eligible-firm share is undefined rather than zero.
e.change_evidence: A validated establishment crosswalk demonstrating privately owned, correctly classified 923120 operating firms with recurring external revenue and LMM EBITDA would be required to estimate eligibility.
caveats: Private contractors serving health departments belong to other NAICS codes and cannot be imported into this record.
The frozen dataset explicitly provides no defensible LMM-band firm count.

s5.rationale: No eligible LMM-firm population is established, and government agencies do not undergo private control transfers comparable to company acquisitions. A qualifying-transfer probability therefore has no defensible denominator.
s5.change_evidence: Evidence of correctly classified private operating firms, followed by owner-age, succession, and control-transaction data for that population, would be needed to estimate this primitive.
caveats: Agency reorganizations, elections, leadership turnover, intergovernmental transfers, and outsourcing contracts are not qualifying control transfers.
Deaths, internal reorganizations, and transactions involving vendors classified elsewhere must remain excluded.

q.rationale: The in-code operators are public agencies without a commercial customer-price mechanism through which an owner retains implemented gross benefit. Appropriation adjustments and public-budget reallocations are not contractual pass-through or competitive repricing under the screen's construct.
q.change_evidence: A documented population of correctly classified private firms with recurring customer contracts, renewal terms, pricing mechanisms, and realized post-automation margins would be required to estimate retention.
caveats: Budget savings may be retained, reappropriated, or used to expand services, but none maps reliably to owner commercial retention.
Applying vendor contract economics would measure industries outside 923120.

d5.bridge: BLS projects essentially flat ten-year employment for government excluding state and local education and hospitals, while projecting 16 percent growth for epidemiologists, many of whom support state and local public-health services. GAO documents persistent workforce gaps and primary jurisdictional responsibility. I interpolate these ten-year employment signals to five years and place the broad service-basket base between the flat government aggregate and the faster-growing epidemiology occupation. Employment is an imperfect proxy for constant-quality service quantity: productivity gains can make it understate demand, while budget-driven staffing and vacancy filling can make it overstate delivered demand.
d5.rationale: Routine public-health administration should remain broadly stable to modestly higher as surveillance, chronic disease, environmental health, preparedness, and data needs persist. The range allows for funding contraction on one side and sustained workforce-gap remediation or emergency needs on the other.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year constant-dollar expenditure and service-volume forecasts for a representative panel of state and local health departments, separated into administration, inspection, surveillance, and emergency-preparedness outputs, would materially change this range.
caveats: Epidemiologists are only one occupation within the industry.
The government-sector projection is much broader than 923120.
Short-term and categorical funding can make realized service quantity volatile even when underlying public-health need is durable.

o.rationale: Software can automate analysis, drafting, intake, and monitoring, but the remaining service basket still requires a government authority to set policy, exercise inspection powers, coordinate emergencies, protect sensitive data, validate scientific outputs, communicate risk, and remain accountable. CDC's AI guidance explicitly requires human oversight and treats generative AI as an aid rather than an authority.
o.change_evidence: Workflow-level operating studies showing which public-health functions can be fully self-served or transferred to software without agency review, statutory sign-off, or accountable intervention would materially change this estimate.
caveats: This is an accountability judgment, not a measured displacement rate.
Statutory authority differs across federal, state, tribal, territorial, and local jurisdictions.
Outsourcing execution does not necessarily remove the accountable public operator.

narrative.executive_view: The operating work has real AI-assistance potential, especially in documents, communications, coding, data preparation, and routine inquiries. The acquisition lens fails at a more basic level: this NAICS code is defined as government establishments, not privately transferable outsourced-service firms.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can shorten drafting, synthesis, translation, communication, coding, surveillance triage, and administrative-support workflows. Scientific interpretation, field inspection, emergency coordination, privacy-sensitive decisions, and public accountability require validation and human ownership, limiting conversion of exposure into implemented substitution.
narrative.value_capture: A public agency can redirect saved staff time, avoid some hiring, or expand service capacity, but it has no ordinary owner margin or customer repricing mechanism. Commercial retention is therefore not measurable for the in-code operator.
narrative.firm_availability: No defensible pool of eligible or transferable LMM firms is established. Private technology, consulting, staffing, laboratory, and technical-assistance vendors may sell recurring services to health departments, but they are outside this industry code.
narrative.demand_durability: Public-health surveillance, program coordination, inspection, preparedness, health statistics, and risk communication remain recurring government functions. Workforce gaps and growing epidemiology needs support durable underlying demand, while appropriations and temporary categorical grants create meaningful quantity volatility.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The decisive risk is category mismatch between a government-only industry and a private control-acquisition screen. Operationally, fragmented systems, procurement, privacy, cybersecurity, model errors, scientific-integrity requirements, and inconsistent jurisdictional capacity can slow implementation; funding contraction can reduce realized service volume despite continuing need.
narrative.principal_driver: Document-heavy, data-heavy public-health administration offers implementable AI assistance while accountable government operation remains necessary.
narrative.principal_weakness: The code contains government establishments and provides no defensible population of privately transferable LMM firms.

sources.title: North American Industry Classification System: Sector 92, NAICS 923120
sources.supports: Defines 923120 as government establishments planning, administering, and coordinating public-health programs and services, including inspections; also identifies hospitals, clinics, and agricultural-product inspection as classifications elsewhere.

sources.title: Considerations for Generative AI in Public Health
sources.supports: Reports public-health agency uses of generative AI for drafting, summaries, code, communications, and chatbots; describes reported time savings and administrative-burden reduction; requires governance, privacy, security, scientific integrity, and human review.

sources.title: Public Health Preparedness: HHS and Jurisdictions Have Taken Some Steps to Address Challenging Workforce Gaps
sources.supports: Finds that more than 3,000 jurisdictional health departments have primary public-health responsibility, estimates 239,000 state and local workers in 2022, documents persistent occupational and operational-support gaps, and explains the constraints of short-term and categorical funding.

sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Epidemiologists
sources.supports: Projects epidemiologist employment to grow 16 percent from 2024 to 2034 and states that many work in state and local governments providing public-health services and emergency response, with employment affected by government budgets.

sources.title: Employment Projections—2024–2034
sources.supports: Projects government employment, excluding state and local education and hospitals, to change by negative 0.1 percent from 2024 to 2034; provides a broad government-sector anchor for demand uncertainty.
